briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for March 18, 2026. Today, we have received various reports on policy responses to climate change, technological developments, and the deepening crisis situation, both domestically and internationally.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Response and Expansion of Carbon Neutrality Education
sections.content: Domestically, policy discussions and educational outreach efforts for climate change response are active. aT President Hong Moon-pyo emphasized the importance of food security through a special lecture on climate change response at the Republic of Korea Constitutional Assembly [1, 3, 4], and the Seoul Metropolitan Area Meteorological Administration and Suwon City signed an MOU to promote climate change science education [5]. The Chungnam Climate and Environment Education Center opened, beginning its role as an environmental education hub for achieving '2045 Carbon Neutrality' [7, 16, 19, 20, 25, 30]. The Korea Environment Corporation is expanding the training of carbon neutrality practical professionals, and Chungcheongnam-do Province is strengthening its policy foundation by operating the '2045 Chungnam Carbon Neutrality' command center [31, 32]. Furthermore, various campaigns encouraging citizen participation are underway, such as Geoje City's 'Visiting Carbon Neutrality Promotion Booth' operation and Gwangju City's launch ceremony for the 'Carbon Neutrality Citizen Promotion Group Green Leaders' [13, 18, 21, 26, 27, 29, 33, 36].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Climate Adaptation Technology Development and Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Efforts to develop climate change adaptation technologies and accelerate the transition to renewable energy are also intensifying. The Korea Rural Community Corporation has unveiled 'K-HAS CS 2.0,' an 'agricultural infrastructure design system' for climate change prediction, and is enhancing its hydraulic and hydrological design systems by adding future flood analysis capabilities, thereby increasing the climate adaptability of agricultural infrastructure [2, 6, 10]. Anseong City's high-quality rice cultivation technology education and Cheongdo County's operation of an emergency pest monitoring and control team are specific examples of agricultural responses [8, 9]. Lotte Mart is shipping 'gas-controlled' apples, striving for stable supply and demand amidst abnormal weather conditions [11]. In terms of energy transition, the enactment of the 'Industrial Green Transition Promotion Act' has signaled the start of a growth-oriented carbon-neutral transition [14]. Siheung City is accelerating its renewable energy transition by securing national funding to expand renewable energy supply and actively supporting the spread of RE100 in industrial complexes [37, 39, 40]. Notably, Korea has achieved a world-record solar cell efficiency of 26.3%, demonstrating its potential to lead in cutting-edge renewable energy technology [42].

sections.title: ⚠️ Deepening Global Climate Crisis and Impacts
sections.content: Globally, warnings about the economic and ecological impacts of climate change continue. A report in Minnesota stated that residents could pay billions of dollars annually if they do not adapt to climate change, and an analysis also indicated that insurance premiums are rising due to climate change [46, 52]. The radical responses of Antarctic penguins to climate change and the uneven reactions of European flora demonstrate the vulnerability of ecosystems [53, 67]. Florida farmers face increased risks from extreme weather [55], and in the UK, the 'underestimated and underreported' risk of wildfires is gaining prominence amidst climate change-induced disruptions [69]. Furthermore, a panel discussion where 'fresh air is a luxury' and a CBS meteorologist's failure to mention climate change when explaining the causes of heatwaves underscore the importance of climate crisis awareness [60, 65]. The news of Ottogi's 90% Scope 3 emissions domestically highlights the urgency for companies to achieve their reduction targets [41].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Change Awareness and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: The international community is focusing on the widespread impacts of climate change and exploring various response strategies. Diverse approaches are being taken, such as finding lessons on climate change in Ovid's 2,000-year-old poem or uncovering the causes of past climate change through ancient ice core research [44, 47]. An analysis suggesting that climate change is redrawing the world's political map [48] and that conflicts act as a hidden cost accelerating climate change demonstrates the importance of understanding the climate crisis from a geopolitical perspective [68]. Research and videos on how Europe's food systems are coping with climate change and conflict have been released [45, 61, 62], and studies have also shown that African cassava crops could face significant risks from climate change [50]. Specific policy and financial approaches are also active, with Maryland initiating a major study on the costs of climate change [63] and discussions in El Salvador about the possibility of introducing climate crisis insurance [66].

briefing.closing: In today's briefing, we have examined the ongoing efforts to address climate change both domestically and internationally, as well as various aspects of the deepening global climate crisis. We must dedicate ourselves to enhancing climate resilience through continuous attention and cooperation. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: ‘They called me a water terrorist’: exiled Iranian scientist wins global prize
articles.description: Prof Kaveh Madani, winner of the Stockholm water prize, was accused of sabotage with his environmental work Eight years before he got the call telling him he had won the Stockholm water prize, Prof Kaveh Madani was being interrogated by Iran’s Revolutionary Guards, accused of being a spy for the CIA, MI6 or the Mossad.Today he is in exile and on Wednesday won the world’s most prestigious water prize for combining “groundbreaking research on water management with policy, diplomacy and global outr

summary.phenomenon: Professor Kaveh Madani of Iran was accused of being a 'water terrorist' and interrogated by the Iranian Revolutionary Guards for his environmental research. Despite currently being in exile, he has been awarded the prestigious Stockholm Water Prize.
summary.cause: Professor Madani's groundbreaking research on water management, combined with his work in policy, diplomacy, and global outreach, was misinterpreted as sabotage by Iranian authorities, leading to the accusations.

articles.description: Long seen as the poor relation to arabica, small growers in the Amazon are rebooting the more resilient robusta’s reputationRead more in the Coffee crisis series hereWhen the Paiter Suruí community expelled the last invaders of their land in 1981, they faced a divisive decision. Should they keep the coffee plantations left by the colonisers? Some destroyed them because of the death and violence contact with the non-Indigenous world had caused. Others felt sorry for the trees and couldn’t kill th

summary.phenomenon: Amidst the global impact on crops due to the climate crisis, small growers in the Brazilian Amazon are rebuilding the reputation of the more resilient Robusta coffee, which is now thriving.
summary.cause: Robusta coffee possesses more resilient characteristics compared to Arabica, a trait particularly relevant given the global impact on crops due to the climate crisis.
